author: @flashlit123
text: Iron (steel wool or rusty nails) and vinegar can make a nice wood stain that seems to be similar to this.  Goes on clear and darkens in a short time.  Probably reacting with the tannin in the wood.

author: @paintedwings74
text:
The trick you've just given about separating out the fats in an emulsion could be exactly the right solution to a problem I'm working out! I've been trying to extract the lipids from a solution of ground velvet-weed seeds, a plentiful and obnoxious weed which has been used for its oil in the past. I consider myself to be an "ancestral technologist," studying and replicating the skills of our ancestors of 5000 to 20,000 years ago. Plant oils can be labor-intensive and difficult to obtain, but their niche as a liquid lipid was a useful one, separate from animal fats.  Ethanol in velvet-weed seed emulsion may be the trick to get it to sort out.  Thoughts?  

Also, I've collected the swim-bladders from carp, knowing these are used both as glue and as "isinglas" fines to make particulates in beer or wine drop out of solution. As a side-tangent, I'd be curious if you have any thoughts on that?

author: @paintedwings74
text:
I make acorn flour every year, using the cold-leaching process that tastes much better and also retains the stickyness of the starches, so it bakes better.  Over the past 7 years, I have it worked out to a rapid "mini-industrial" process, by which I mean I can turn out 20 L in the amount of time most people, doing it as an experiment, turn out one or two. If you're interested in the details, please let me know, I'd be happy to describe; though I know you're primarily focused on the ink. (I wish I could separate out the sugars from the first soak of red oak acorns ... the liquid smells like maple syrup, but oh-so-much better.)  

It's not just the Americas that used acorns as a staple food, by the way; Five of the seven continents relied on acorns as one of their food sources. This year, in Wisconsin, USA, our acorn crop and hickory crops were both terrible, city-wide, which illustrates why any given nut would be gathered and stored every time they were highly productive or of exceptional quality. Yes, Great Britain's Neolithic people may have eaten hazelnuts in great quantities, but there would be years the hazel crop would fail; and there would be their slightly more effortful acorns to back up their winter food stores.  The chestnuts of the East Coast of the US would likewise have been the number-one source of starch and fats in their range, but acorns were a backup; and then there's the secondary calories of eating the squirrels, deer, turkeys (US), wild boar (UK), and pigeons which consumed any particular set of nuts. 

I really appreciate the iron-gall ink video, by the way--I've been collecting galls for several years to do that, but never got 'round to trying it. Steel wool in HCl to saturation, that's easy enough!

author: @larrystrayer8336
text: In our heritage club we just take acorns ( red and water oak) leave them whole. Boil for 4 + hours     Drain off the liquid then reduce by boiling by 2/3 and use to tan hides.  And reduced to a syrup use as a wood stain when mixed with oil.  Strong tannic

text:
A new subscriber and a huge fan from syria here 
Wanted to say im addicted to ur vids and i love ur spirit u really are an inspiration.
My father once mentioned to me when i was a young boy that  Cupressus sempervirens was used to extract ink in the old days 
Also a link down to an article about how ancient arabs calygraphers used to make there ink
Its in arabic soooo hope google translator works but from what i read its about the same technique u use and also there were no standard measurements.
Thanks for ur lovely videos and most importantly ur lovely spirit.
Bassel.

author: @markuslanz8667
text:
As always, very nice video, I love all this stuff about taking basic natural ingredients and turning them into usable products, like your dirt capacitor or this ink.
 Now my question; I know how to make a high ph solution with wood ash for example, but is there a way to use natural ingredients in order to make an acidic solution, or even hydrochloric acid? It would be great if you could help me, and thank you for these videos!

replies.author: @mikeguitar9769
replies.text:
Well, there’s:

Carbonated water

Lemon juice

Vinegar

author: @ianbutler1983
text: A pointer I learned the hard way. Do not have open Hydrochloric act in your shop. Fast rust will form on any iron or steel object in the vicinity. I did not believe it, but then.........

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: personally I keep the acids according to coshh and reach - but nice tip cheers mate

replies.author: @ianbutler1983
@mikeguitar9769
replies.text: Yeah, HCl is a gas.  (Has low vapor pressure).  The gas will leave the liquid solution, just like a carbonated soda going “flat”.

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: @grandpaseed Lots of effort to collect and burn what is primarily a food.  What would make it a good biofuel is the use of the waste product.  One could remove the "flour" to use as food and still burn the waste cellulose and oil for heat or  light.

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I don't think evergreen live oak are native to the sunny shores of your island, and these can provide more than one "crop" per year, as well as galls, in the American SW (also, I am told, in Central and temperate South America).  We also have mesquite in the Americas which can be used for food and probably for tannic acid.
